coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on nanofiber membranes as high-efficiency photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Huanhuan
Xu, Lan
Ahmed, Adnan
coredata.dc:description: Nano semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted more and more attention and developed rapidly because of their high degradation efficiency, low energy consumption and non-toxic and harmless in sewage treatment. In this work, a simple three-step preparation method combining electrospinning, heat treatment and hydrothermal synthesis was applied to obtain carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on nanofiber membranes (NFMs), which were composed of CQDs-decorated ZnO nanorods. The morphology, structure, chemical composition and properties of nanocomposites were characterized by using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer and fluorescence spectrometer, determining the formation of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers and the optimum experimental parameters. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism was further revealed by the Mott-Schottky test and active radical capture experiment. The results demonstrated that when the addition amount of CQD solution was 300 μL, the produced NFMs with CQDs-decorated ZnO had the best light absorption performance, leading to its best degradation effect on methylene blue (degradation efficiency over 88 %, 4 h) under natural sunlight irradiation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospinning
Hydrothermal
ZnO/CQDs
Photocatalysis
Methylene blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures grown on NFMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures grown on NFMs.
SEM pictures of CNFMs with varied CQD amounts: (a) CZ-0; (b) CZ-1; (c) CZ-2; (d) CZ-3; (e)CZ-4; (f) CZ-5.
(a) TEM picture of CQDs (inset: HRTEM picture of CQDs); (b, c) TEM pictures and (d) HRTEM picture of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of CQDs and CNFMs with different CQD amounts.
XPS spectra of CNFMs with varied CQD amounts: (a) Survey spectra; (b) C1s spectra; (c) O 1s spectra; (d) Zn 2p spectra.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (b) (αhv)2-hv fitting curves and (c) PL spectra of the CNFMs with different CQDs addition.
(a) Cyclic degradation curve of CZ-3; (b) Degradation curves of MB by CZ-0 and CZ-3 under natural sunlight.
(a) Mott-Schottky curves of CZ-0 and CZ-3; (b) Results of free radical capture experiments.
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure.
Photocatalytic degradation rates of MB by CNFMs with different CQD amounts.
Photocatalytic degradation activity of ZnO-based NFMs.

row.entry:
UV light: 60 % for 3 h,
94.2 % for 7 h
Visible light: 72 % for 3 h, 85 % for 4 h, 98 % for 6 h
lanthanum doped ZnO on PAN NFM

UV light: 92.08 % for 3 h,
Sunlight: 88.36 % for 4 h

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on nanofiber membranes as high-efficiency photocatalysts

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk, College of Textile and Engineering, Soochow University, 199 Ren-ai Road, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Modern Silk, College of Textile and Engineering, Soochow University, 199 Ren-ai Road, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Engineering Research Center of Textile Dyeing and Printing for Energy Conservation, Discharge Reduction and Cleaner Production (ERC), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Research Center of Textile Dyeing and Printing for Energy Conservation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Engineering Research Center of Textile Dyeing and Printing for Energy Conservation, Discharge Reduction and Cleaner Production (ERC), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted more and more attention and developed rapidly because of their high degradation efficiency, low energy consumption and non-toxic and harmless in sewage treatment. In this work, a simple three-step preparation method combining electrospinning, heat treatment and hydrothermal synthesis was applied to obtain carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on nanofiber membranes (NFMs), which were composed of CQDs-decorated ZnO nanorods. The morphology, structure, chemical composition and properties of nanocomposites were characterized by using scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer and fluorescence spectrometer, determining the formation of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers and the optimum experimental parameters. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism was further revealed by the Mott-Schottky test and active radical capture experiment. The results demonstrated that when the addition amount of CQD solution was 300 μL, the produced NFMs with CQDs-decorated ZnO had the best light absorption performance, leading to its best degradation effect on methylene blue (degradation efficiency over 88 %, 4 h) under natural sunlight irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, toxic and refractory organic pollutants have caused serious environmental problems [1,2]. Photocatalytic technology has gotten a lot of interest and progressed quickly due to its advantages of high degradation efficiency, simple operation, low energy consumption and no secondary pollution [3–5]. The core of photocatalytic technology is photocatalyst [6], among which nano semiconductor photocatalysts, such as SnO2, ZnO and TiO2, have been widely concerned and researched because of their high efficiency, low energy consumption, non-toxic and harmless in sewage treatment [7–10]. ZnO has a band gap of 3.37 eV, which is an n-type semiconductor [11]. It possesses stable physical and chemical properties, excellent photoelectric properties, non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, and low price. Therefore, ZnO has become a highly active photocatalyst with great development prospects [12–14]. ZnO nanomaterials with different morphologies obtained by different preparation methods have been widely applied in photocatalysis because of their nano sizes and high specific surface areas [15,16].

However, ZnO has some limitations as a photocatalyst. For example, its wide band gap limits its utilization of sunlight [17], its quantum efficiency is very low, and the electron hole pairs on its surface are easy to recombine. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel type of luminescent carbon nanomaterial, which have the advantages of diverse sources, low cost and toxicity, stable performances, good biocompatibility and anti-photobleaching [18]. In addition, CQDs have the ability of fluorescence up-conversion, photoinduced electron transfer and photosensitivity, which enables them to be applied in the field of photocatalysis [19]. Therefore, the integration of ZnO and CQDs can convert the absorbed long wavelength light into short wavelength light, forming more photogenerated electron-hole pairs on ZnO. Moreover, the conjugated π bond of CQDs is conducive to promoting the adsorption of organic pollutants on ZnO surface, thus improving the photocatalytic activity of ZnO [20]. ZnO/CQDs composite nanomaterials have been synthesized in some studies, but they are nanoparticle composites, which are difficult to recover and reuse, especially [20–23].

ZnO/CQDs loaded composite nanofiber membranes (CNFMs) can not only overcome the defect that ZnO/CQDs nanoparticle photocatalysts are not easy to be recovered, but also avoid the aggregation and wastage of nanoparticles in the reaction procedure, so that they can be reused without polluting the environment. Electrospinning (ES) is easy to operate and can directly, continuously and effectively fabricate nanofiber membranes (NFMs). The prepared NFMs have the characteristics of large specific surface area, uniform diameter distribution, and easy recycling [24–27]. The CNFMs containing ZnO nanomaterials with various morphologies and structures prepared by ES have a wide range of applications in the field of photocatalysis [28–36].

In this paper, CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on NFMs were synthesized by ES, thermal treatment and hydrothermal method, possessing larger specific surface area and more photocatalytic active sites. The morphology, structure, chemical composition and properties of the nanocomposites were characterized to determine the optimum experimental parameters. Furthermore, these nanocomposites were used for photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB), exhibiting enhanced photocatalytic degradation property. Meanwhile, the Mott-Schottky test and active radical capture experiments illustrated the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs-decorated ZnO, revealing that loading appropriate amount CQDs could expand the light utilization range of ZnO, enhance the photoinduced electron transfer, and effectively separate the photogenerated electron hole pairs, thus improving the photocatalytic efficiency of photocatalysts.

Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, 150,000 g/mol, AR) were obtained from Hefei Sipin Technol. Co., Ltd. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Mw = 73.09 g/mol, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. Zn(Ac)2 (Mw = 183.48 g/mol, AR) was offered from Aladdin Biochem. Co., Ltd. Graphite rods were purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., Ltd. ZnCl2 (136.30 g/mol, CP), NH3•H2O (17.03 g/mol, GR), Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA, 140.19 g/mol, AR) were provided from China Pharmaceutical Group Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. Methylene blue (373.90 g/mol) were supplied by Shanghai Debai Biotech. Co., Ltd. Na2SO4 (142.04 g/mol), Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 60.01 g/mol) was received from Qiangsheng Functional Chem. Co., Ltd. P-benzoquinone (BQ, 108.1 g/mol, 99 %) was obtained from Suzhou Grete Pharmaceutical Technol. Co., Ltd. Ammoniumq oxalate (AO, 142.11 g/mol, 99.99 %) was provided from Jiangsu Argon Krypton Xenon Material Technol. Co., Ltd. The mentioned above reagents were used without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures grown on NFMs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures grown on NFMs were fabricated by an incorporation of ES, thermal treatment method and hydrothermal synthesis, as exhibited in Fig. 1 .

Firstly, CQD solution were synthesized by electrochemical etching of graphite rods [37]. Then, 1 mL of the prepared CQD solution was put into a 5 mL centrifuge tube, and 2 mL of deionized water was added into the tube. Finally, the solution was sonicated for 10 min to obtain the diluted and dispersed CQD solution for standby.

Secondly, DMF was used as solvent and PAN was used as solute to prepare a PAN solution with a concentration of 8 wt%. Then, the solution was transferred to a collector magnetic stirrer for heating and stirring at 80 °C for 2 h, and the solution was completely dissolved until it was uniform and transparent [38]. Next, a certain mass of Zn(Ac)2 powder (the mass ratio of Zn(Ac)2 to PAN was 1:2) was added into the above solution. Finally, the mixed solution was stirred magnetically at 25 ± 2 °C until a uniformly dispersed spinning solution was formed.

Thirdly, the ES experiments were performed, and the spinning parameters were set as follows: the working distance was 15 cm, the flow rate was 0.6 mL/h, the voltage was 15 kV, the laboratory temperature was controlled at 25 ± 2 °C and the humidity was 50 ± 5 %. The electrospun Zn(Ac)2/PAN CNFMs were cut into 3 cm × 3 cm pieces and put in Muffle furnace for heat treatment at 140 °C for 3 h, resulting in the formation of ZnO nanoparticles.

Finally, the heat-treated CNFMs were placed into the reactor including the clarified and transparent growth solution, which was synthesized from the mixture of ZnCl2, NH3 •H2O, HMTA and deionized water. The molar mass ratio of ZnCl2 to HMTA was 1:1, the volume of NH3 •H2O added was 5 % relative to the growth solution, while the volume proportion of growth solution to reactor was 1:5. Afterwards, different amounts of CQD solutions (100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μL) were added into the reactors, respectively. These reactors were put into the blast drying oven at 90 °C for 3 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the CNFMs were taken out of reactors, washed with deionized water and dried to obtain CNFMs loaded with CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures. These obtained CNFMs with different CQD amounts were named as CZ-0 (without CQDs), CZ-1 (100 μL), CZ-2 (200 μL), CZ-3 (300 μL), CZ-4 (400 μL) and CZ-5 (500 μL), separately.

The morphologies of samples were examined through a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Regulus 8100, Japan). The CQDs and their distribution on ZnO surface were observed by a transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) patterns of samples were recorded by a FTIR spectroscopy (Nicolet5700, USA). The crystal structures of CNFMs were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Germany). The chemical composition and bonding environment were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, UK). The light absorption capacity measurements of CNFMs were performed using a UV–Vis-NIR Spectrometer (UV3600, Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CNFMs was analyzed by a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS920, Japan). The Mott-Schottky tests were carried out by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China) to exhibit the energy band positions of nanocomposites.

The photocatalytic activities of CNFMs were tested through degrading MB solutions (10 mg/L) under an UV lamp (250 W, 365 nm) and natural sunlight, respectively. In the dark enviroment, 0.1 g CNFMs were immersed in the 40 mL MB solution for 1 h to reach adsorption-desorption balance. The MB solution was analyzed through a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 664 nm to record its maximum absorbance at different time intervals. The degradation rate (η) was calculated by the following formula:
η
%
=
Absorbance of the original solution − Absorbance of the current solution
Absorbance of the original solution
× 100 %

Free radical capture experiments were used to explore the major active components in the photocatalytic degradation of MB. To capture photoproduced holes (h+), superoxide radicals (·O2 −), and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), 0.01 M of AO, BQ, and IPA were added into the MB solution, separately.

Fig. 2 showed SEM pictures of CNFMs with varied CQD amounts. It was found CZ-0 have regular shape, smooth surface and uniform size. With the increase of CQD amounts, the size of CQDs-decorated ZnO nanoflowers changed little due to too small size of CQDs, but their shape began to become irregular and uneven. Especially when the added CQD solution was 400 μL and 500 μL, there was obvious adhesion between the CQDs-decorated ZnO nanoflowers, which made the shape of nanoflowers more irregular and the surface of nanoflowers more rough, indicating that the greater the amount of CQDs, the greater the influence on the growth of CQDs-decorated ZnO nanoflowers.

The microstructures of the synthesized CQDs and CQDs-decorated ZnO were further characterized by TEM and HRTEM, as exhibited in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) and its inset displayed that CQDs had a particle size distribution in the range of 5–10 nm, and the CQD nanoparticles had good dispersion without obvious aggregation. Fig. 3(b-d) showed the nanorods that made up the CQDs-decorated ZnO nanoflowers. It was found that the surface of ZnO nanorods was rough and filled with CQD spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 3(b, c)). The lattice spacing of CQDs was 0.21 nm, which was consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphite phase [39], while the lattice spacing of ZnO was 0.26 nm, assigning to the (002) crystal face of ZnO (hexagonal wurtzite) [40] (Fig. 3(d)). The results revealed that the nanoflowers shown in SEM pictures (Fig. 2) were composed of ZnO nanorods and CQD spherical nanoparticles, which were CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructures.

In order to further confirm the presence of CQDs in CNFMs and study the interaction between ZnO and CQDs, the chemical structure and composition of samples were analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4(a), CQDs (3346 cm−1) and all CNFMs (3360 cm−1) exhibited a wide absorption band around 3400 cm−1, which was the stretching vibration of -OH. In the FTIR spectrum of CQDs, the absorption peak of 1634 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of carboxyl group CO [41], while the peak of 1108 cm−1 was assigned to the vibration of oxygen-containing group. In the FTIR spectra of CNFMs, the typical characteristic peak near 450 cm−1 corresponded to ZnO bond of ZnO [42]. However, with the increase of CQD amounts, the characteristic peak intensity of ZnO relatively weakened, which might be due to the excessive load of CQDs, so that ZnO on the surface of CNFMs could not be fully exposed. Simultaneously, the FTIR spectra of all CNFMs showed absorption peaks at 2242 cm−1 and 1733 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration peaks of CN and CO in PAN, separately. The characteristic peak of 1452 cm−1 belonged to the symmetric stretching vibration peak of -COO- in Zn(Ac)2. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1678 cm−1, 1362 cm−1 and 1062 cm−1 were assigned to the vibration of CO, symmetric carboxylic acid and CO, respectively [43]. Compared with CQDs, the peaks assigned to CQDs in the CNFMs had a slight blue shift, which might be due to the external interaction between ZnO and CQDs [43].

The crystal structures of CNFMs with different CQD amounts were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the nine characteristic diffraction peaks of ZnO were entirely visible in the XRD patterns of all CNFMs, which respectively corresponded to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201) crystal planes of ZnO (hexagonal wurtzite) (JCPDS No. 36–1451), demonstrating that the obtained ZnO was pure and impurity-free. There was also an obvious diffraction peak at 2θ = 17° in these XRD patterns, which belonged to the (110) crystal plane of PAN [29]. The diffraction peaks of CNFMs with and without CQDs were basically the same except for a slight red shift of the peak position, and there were no diffraction peaks identified in the CQD, which could be attributed to its too small content or amorphous structure [41]. The results illustrated that the addition of CQDs had no significant effect on the crystal structure of nanocomposites. However, the diffraction peak intensity of ZnO in CZ-5 was obviously weaker than that of other CNFMs, which was caused by the relatively large load of CQD.

As exhibited in Fig. 5 , the chemical composition and bonding environment of CNFMs with different amounts of CQDs were analyzed by XPS. It could be found the CNFMs mainly included four elements: C, N, O and Zn (Fig. 5(a)). C 1s spectra (Fig. 5(b)) had three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.3 eV, corresponding to sp2 C=C/C-C, CO and CO, separately, and the amount of chemical bonds corresponding to the three peaks of C 1s in CNFMs with varied CQD amounts was different. O 1s spectra (Fig. 5(c)) focused on the two peaks of 530.2 eV and 532.0 eV, which were related to the lattice oxygen in ZnO and the oxygen in O-C=O, respectively, indicating that the oxygen in CZ-0 and CZ-3 was dominated by the lattice oxygen in ZnO, while the oxygen in CZ-5 was mainly the oxygen in O-C=O, which was due to the coating effect of the higher amount of CQDs on ZnO. Zn 2p spectra (Fig. 5(d)) showed that two obvious peaks were concentrated at 1021.1 eV and 1044.2 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 separately, thus reflecting that Zn existed in the CNFMs in the form of Zn2+.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNFMs with different CQD amounts (Fig. 6(a)) showed that the light absorption intensity of CNFMs loaded with CQDs was enhanced, which was because CQDs with nano size (<10 nm) could fully contact with ZnO and form a tight nano heterostructure, so that the charge transfer time and distance were shortened, thus accelerating the charge transfer between interfaces. When the amount of CQDs was 300 μL, the light absorption intensity of CZ-3 was the highest. When the higher amount of CQDs was loaded on ZnO, the area of ZnO exposed to light would be reduced, resulting in the reduction of light absorption intensity. Therefore, an appropriate loading of CQDs could avoid the shading effect, ensuring that CQDs-decorated ZnO could make full use of the light source.

According to Kubelka-Munk equation, the band gap energy (BGE) of CNFMs could be determined [44]. The curves of (αhv)2-hv were drawn and their tangent lines in the high absorption range were intersected with the X-axis, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The coordinates of the intersection points were the BGE values of samples. It could be found that the BGE value of CZ-3 was the smallest, while the BGE value of CZ-5 was the largest and higher than that of CZ-0, which also showed that an appropriate amount of CQDs had the potential to minimize the band gap of ZnO, extend the light absorption spectrum, and enhance the solar energy utilization.

The PL intensity of photocatalyst is closely connected to its optical characteristics, and its PL spectrum can be used to analyze the charge separation and migration efficiency between the photogenerated carriers of samples. For example, the sample with relatively strong fluorescence intensity has a high recombination rate of photoproduced electron-hole pairs, leading to its relatively weak photocatalytic activity. The PL spectra of CNFMs with different CQD amounts were shown in Fig. 6(c) (excitation wavelength was 350 nm). It could be seen that except for CZ-5, the PL intensity of other CNFMs loaded with CQDs was significantly lower than that of CZ-0, which could be attributed to the effective electron transfer produced by the π conjugated system of CQDs [45]. This indicated that CQDs could significantly inhibit the recombination of photoproduced electrons and holes, improve the transient photoelectron response of ZnO and reduce the PL intensity of ZnO. Among them, CZ-3 showed the weakest fluorescence intensity, indicating that it had longer carrier lifetime and lower recombination rate of photoproduced electron-hole pairs. However, the PL intensity of CZ-5 was higher than that of CZ-0 because the higher amount of CQDs hindered the electron transport of ZnO.

The photocatalytic degradation of organic dye MB by CNFMs with different CQD amounts was carried out to explore the photocatalytic activity of these CNFMs. As displayed in Table 1 , the degradation rate of CNFMs under mercury lamp first enhanced and then reduced as the amount of CQD increased. Among them, the degradation rate of CZ-3 was the highest, reaching 92.08 % after 3 h, which was due to the formation of ZnO/CQDs heterojunction and the good dispersion of CQDs. However, the degradation rate of CZ-5 was lower than that of CZ-0, which was because the excessive load of CQDs without photocatalytic ability hindered the light capture and charge transfer capability of pure ZnO nanoflowers, thus reducing the photocatalytic performance. Therefore, it was very important to reasonably control the load of CQDs on CNFMs. In summary, when the addition amount of CQDs was 300 μL, CZ-3 had the best properties.

Three cycle degradation studies were carried out to further examine the recycling of CZ-3 with the highest photocatalytic effect, and the results were given in Fig. 7(a). It could be found that after three cycles of degradation, CZ-3 still maintained a stable photocatalytic activity, and the degradation rate of MB solution was not significantly reduced, which could still reach >90 %, proving that CZ-3 had good photocatalytic stability.

In addition, CZ-0 and CZ-3 were subjected to photocatalytic degradation experiments of MB under natural sunlight, and their degradation rates were dispayed in Fig. 7(b). The degradation rates of CZ-0 and CZ-3 reached 77.81 % and 88.36 % separately after degrading MB for 4 h under natural sunlight, indicating that the CNFMs still had a good photocatalytic degradation effect under natural sunlight. Moreover, the photocatalytic performance of CZ-3 was better than that of CZ-0, implying that CQDs expanded the absorption range of ZnO to sunlight. Meanwhile, compared with other reported ZnO-based NFMs, CZ-3 also exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity, as shown in Table 2 .

The type of semiconductor photocatalyst and its conduction band location (ECB) can be determined by Mott-Schottky tests. As illustrated in Fig. 8(a), the slopes of the Mott-Schottky curves of CZ-0 and CZ-3 were positive, proving that they were n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potential (EFB) of CZ-0 and CZ-3 obtained from X-axis tangent intercepts was −0.55 V and − 0.6 V, respectively. According to ECB = EFB-0.2, ECB of CZ-0 and CZ-3 was −0.75 V and − 0.8 V, respectively. The lower the ECB of the sample, the stronger its electron reduction ability.

Free radical capture studies were carried out in the degradation stage to further clarify the photocatalytic degradation mechanism. In the presence of various capture agents, the photocatalytic degradation effects of CZ-3 were exhibited in Fig. 8(b). It could be found that after adding AO and BQ respectively, the degradation rates of MB could still reach 83.55 % and 82.66 %, which meant that h+ and ·O2 − had little effect on the degradation process. However, after the introduction of IPA, due to its capture of ·OH, the degradation rate of MB was very small, only 37.76 %, indicating that ·OH was the main active group for the degradation of MB compared with h+ and ·O2 −.

The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure is shown in Fig. 9 . The electron (e−) on the valence band (VB) of ZnO in the nanocomposite photocatalyst will be transferred to the conduction band (CB) by the excitation of UV–Vis light, and the photogenerated electron-hole pairs will be generated. Meanwhile, due to its up-conversion fluorescence properties, CQDs can absorb long wavelength light and convert it into short wavelength light, thus further exciting ZnO to form more photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, the photoinduced electron transfer property of CQDs can affect the electron distribution of ZnO, so that the e− on the CB of ZnO migrates to the CQD surface to form a secondary structure, thus accelerating the effective separation of photoproduced electron-hole pairs and interacting with oxygen molecules to generate ·O2 −. The resultant ·O2 − can react with organic pollutants. Moreover, the h+ on the CB of ZnO can oxidize OH− and H2O into more ·OH to participate in the degradation of organic pollutants.

In summary, CQDs-decorated ZnO heterostructure nanoflowers grown on NFMs were successfully fabricated by ES, thermal treatment and hydrothermal method. The influences of the addition amount of CQDs solution on the morphologies, structures and properties of CNFMs were discussed. The morphology and structure characterization of CNFMs illustrated that when the added CQD solution was 300 μL, the shape of the CQDs-decorated ZnO nanoflowers on CZ-3 remained regular and uniform, which were heterostructures composed of ZnO nanorods and CQD spherical nanoparticles. The UV–Vis and PL analysis of CNFMs showed that CZ-3 had the best light absorption ability and the highest separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Accordingly, the photocatalytic degradation experiments revealed that CZ-3 had the best photocatalytic activity and good recyclability, and its photocatalytic degradation efficiency for MB solution exceeded 92 % under mercury lamp for 3 h and 88 % under natural sunlight irradiation for 4 h. Eventually, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs-decorated ZnO was explored and further revealed by Mott-Schottky tests and free radical capture experiments, which displayed that CQDs-decorated ZnO was still a n-type semiconductor, and ·OH was the major active group for the degradation of MB.

Huanhuan Xu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Lan Xu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Adnan Ahmed: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11672198), Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant No. 20KJA130001), Jiangsu Engineering Research Center of Textile Dyeing and Printing for Energy Conservation (Grant No. ERC-Q811580722), and PAPD (A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments of zinc oxide based photocatalyst in water treatment technology: a review
Water Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] K.M. Lee, C.W. Lai, K.S. Ngai, J.C. Juan, Recent developments of zinc oxide based photocatalyst in water treatment technology: A review, Water Res. 88 (2016) 428-448.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic optical fibers for degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater: a review
Environ. Chem. Lett.

Chang, Photocatalytic optical fibers for degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater: a review, Environ. Chem. Lett. 19 (2021) 1335-1346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties of ga doped and ag doped ZnO nanorods for water treatment
Catalysts.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [3] M.N. Cardoza-Contreras, A. Vasquez-Gallegos, A. Vidal-Limon, J.M. Romo-Herrera, S. Aguila, O.E. Contreras, Photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties of Ga doped and Ag doped ZnO nanorods for water treatment, Catalysts. 9 (2019).
